one_line_takeaway: A unified benchmark (RSB) re-evaluating 13 RAG poisoning attacks and 7 defenses over 15 datasets shows attacks that dominate standard QA benchmarks largely collapse once the knowledge base is dense with correct answers, while no existing defense offers robust protection.
key_finding: RAG poisoning is highly effective on standard QA datasets but is passively neutralized by information density. On original NQ, prompt-injection attacks reach ASR 0.94 (BPI) and 0.97 (WPI), CRAG-AS 0.89, and AgentPoison 0.99, but on the large-expansion datasets ASR collapses to near zero (e.g., NQ-EX-L: BPI 0.08, WPI 0.10, most others 0.00-0.05) because more retrieved correct-answer texts drown out poisoned ones. The exception is CorruptRAG-AK, whose per-text budget optimization sustains ASR on expansions (NQ-EX-M 0.66, MS-MARCO-EX-L 0.44, SQuAD-EX-L 0.33). Defenses are inconsistent: process-optimized methods (InstructRAG, AstuteRAG) crush DoS attacks (JamOracle ASR 0.87->0.00/0.01 on NQ) but barely dent targeted poisoning; detection defenses PPL/Norm are essentially useless (BPI ASR stays 0.94); the hybrid TrustRAG is the strongest (BPI 0.94->0.03, WPI 0.97->0.05) yet only partially mitigates. An LLM ablation finds Claude markedly more robust than GPT/Gemini/DeepSeek/Llama peers (e.g., under BPI it holds ACC 0.28 and cuts ASR to 0.65 vs ~0.90+ for others). Poisons transfer to advanced RAG architectures, but adaptive-retrieval frameworks like FLARE resist by skipping unnecessary retrieval; multi-turn RAG is harder to poison due to query rewriting; multimodal RAG and RAG-based agents remain vulnerable.
core_contribution: The paper introduces RAG Security Bench (RSB), the first unified benchmark for systematically evaluating knowledge-database poisoning attacks and defenses against Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). RSB standardizes threat models and metrics across 13 representative poisoning attacks (grouped into targeted poisoning, denial-of-service, and trigger-based DoS), 7 defenses (process-optimized, detection-based, hybrid), and 15 QA datasets (5 standard + 10 challenging 'expanded' variants), and extends evaluation to 6 advanced RAG frameworks (sequential, branching, conditional, loop) plus multi-turn conversational RAG, multimodal RAG, and RAG-based LLM agents. Its central empirical contribution is showing that (a) attacks that succeed on standard QA datasets collapse once the knowledge base is enriched with more correct-answer texts, and (b) existing defenses do not robustly protect RAG. NOTE: the 13 attacks (PoisonedRAG/BPRAG-WPRAG, BPI/WPI prompt injection, AGGD, CorruptRAG, Jamming attacks, AgentPoison, BadRAG, Phantom) and the 7 defenses (InstructRAG, RobustRAG, AstuteRAG, TrustRAG, etc.) are all PRIOR work re-implemented and re-evaluated by the benchmark; the paper's own contribution is the unified benchmark, the expanded datasets, and the cross-cutting findings, not the individual attacks/defenses.
threat_model: Attacker injects arbitrary malicious text into the RAG knowledge database (e.g., by editing Wikipedia-style public sources that feed the database) so that poisoned entries are later retrieved and steer the LLM's output. The attacker has NO access to LLM internal parameters and CANNOT modify user queries (that would be jailbreaking). For the retriever, both black-box (no access) and white-box (read-only parameter access, no modification) settings are considered; the attacker may or may not know the knowledge-base contents or the targeted queries. Three attacker objectives: targeted poisoning (force a specific attacker-chosen answer for a target query, e.g., answer 'Tim Cook' to 'Who is the CEO of OpenAI?'), denial-of-service (force refusal like 'I don't know' for target queries), and trigger-based DoS (force refusal for any query containing a predefined trigger phrase).
limitations: The authors note their agent evaluation is limited to RAG-based agents and does not cover more complex MCP-based agents (flagged as future work). Reproduction caveats: BPRAG/WPRAG and BadRAG/Phantom underperform their original papers' reported numbers, attributed to LLM-setting differences (the original PoisonedRAG used now-deprecated PaLM 2, forcing re-runs). All ASR/ACC judgments rely on GPT-4o-mini as an automated judge, and the default pipeline fixes one retriever (Contriever), one judge, and K=5, so absolute numbers are configuration-dependent. The 'passive defense' via database enrichment is an observed side effect of dataset expansion rather than a deployable, adversary-aware defense, and no genuinely robust defense is demonstrated.

datasets_used: 10 expanded variants (EX-M and EX-L for each of the 5 datasets), for 15 total QA datasets
100 unified targeted queries/answers per attack category
quantitative_results: Original NQ ASR: BPI 0.94, WPI 0.97, CRAG-AS 0.89, CRAG-AK 0.88, JamInject 0.85, JamOracle 0.87, AGGD 0.51, BPRAG 0.62, WPRAG 0.64, AgentPoison(AP) 0.99, BadRAG 0.35, Phantom 0.00
NQ-EX-L ASR collapse: BPI 0.08, WPI 0.10, BPRAG 0.03, CRAG-AS 0.00, CRAG-AK 0.20, most DoS/trigger attacks 0.00-0.02
CRAG-AK sustains ASR on expansions: NQ-EX-M 0.66, MS-MARCO-EX-M 0.66, MS-MARCO-EX-L 0.44, SQuAD-EX-L 0.33
Defenses on NQ (ASR): TrustRAG cuts BPI 0.94->0.03 and WPI 0.97->0.05; InstructRAG/AstuteRAG cut JamOracle 0.87->0.00/0.01; PPL and Norm leave BPI at 0.94 (negligible effect)
LLM ablation on NQ: Claude far more robust than peers (BPI ACC 0.28 and ASR 0.65 under Claude vs BPI ACC ~0.01-0.07 and ASR ~0.88-0.98 for GPT-4o-mini/GPT-4o/GPT-4.1/Gemini/DeepSeek/Llama-4)
Dot-product similarity found more vulnerable than cosine similarity, especially under white-box attacks
Default config: top-K=5, 5 runs averaged, GPT-4o-mini as LLM and as ASR/ACC judge
baselines_compared: Targeted poisoning attacks: BPRAG, WPRAG, BPI, WPI, AGGD, CorruptRAG-AS (CRAG-AS), CorruptRAG-AK (CRAG-AK)
DoS attacks: JamInject, JamOracle, JamOpt (Jamming/blocker-document attacks)
Trigger-based DoS attacks: AgentPoison (AP), BadRAG, Phantom
Defenses: Paraphrasing, InstructRAG, RobustRAG, AstuteRAG (process-optimized); PPL, Norm (detection-based); TrustRAG (hybrid)
Retrievers: Contriever (default) plus two other state-of-the-art retrievers; 10 LLMs including GPT-4o-mini/GPT-4o/GPT-4.1 family, Gemini, Claude, DeepSeek-R1/V3, Llama-4
Advanced RAG: sequential, branching, conditional, loop (6 frameworks incl. FLARE); plus multi-turn, multimodal (Poisoned-MRAG), and RAG-based LLM agents
